preFix.title: Building a straw-bale compost enclosure
preFix.subtitle: A two-bay compost structure using straw bales as insulated, self-composting walls
preFix.excerpt: A three-sided straw-bale enclosure creates an insulated compost bay that generates and retains heat more effectively than a pallet or wire-mesh bin. The bales themselves decompose over two to three years, adding to the compost output. For a large garden with plenty of material, a two-bay straw-bale system is significantly more productive than any plastic tumbler.
preFix.sourceNotes: Centre for Alternative Technology (CAT) composting and soil fertility guides. Garden Organic composting guidance. Charles Dowding no-dig growing and composting resources (public). RHS guidance on hot composting enclosures.

content.text: A straw bale holds air within its structure, which insulates the heap and retains the heat generated by decomposition. Whereas a wire-mesh bin loses heat rapidly on cold nights and may never reach the 55°C threshold for
hot composting
, a straw-bale enclosure holds that heat overnight and through cold spells. The composting season in a straw-bale bin is effectively two to three months longer than in an uninsulated structure.
Materials for a two-bay structure
12-16 standard barley or wheat straw bales (approximately 450 x 350 x 750 mm). Barley straw is denser and lasts longer; wheat straw decomposes faster and contributes more to the eventual compost.
8 wooden stakes, 50 mm x 50 mm x 900 mm, for pinning the bales.
Optional: a 900 mm wide pallet or length of timber for the front face of each bay, to allow height adjustment as the heap is built and turned.

Composting preamble: wear garden gloves when turning the heap. The partially decomposed material in an active heap contains thermophilic bacteria and mould spores; the risk to healthy adults is low, but individuals sensitive to mould should wear an FFP2 dust mask when turning.
Layout and construction

Choose a level site with access from at least one side for a wheelbarrow. Mark out a two-bay footprint: two bays each 900 mm x 900 mm internally, with a shared central wall. The total footprint is approximately 2 m x 1 m for the bale walls plus 1 m of access.
Lay the back wall: three bales end-on in a line. The bales butt tightly against each other. No mortar or bonding needed; the weight and friction hold them.
Lay the side walls: one bale per side of each bay, laid lengthwise. These run perpendicular to the back wall.
Lay a second course staggered over the first (offset by half a bale length) to interlock the corners.
Drive stakes through each bale vertically to pin the first and second courses together, one stake per corner and one per length of wall.
Leave the front of each bay open for loading and turning. A removable pallet board or loose bale can close the front once the bay is loaded.
Loading and turning
Fill bay 1 with a well-balanced mix: aim for a
C:N ratio

of 25-30:1 with alternating layers of green (grass clippings, kitchen scraps, fresh weeds) and brown material (dry leaves, cardboard, straw). Wet the heap as you build, to the texture of a wrung-out sponge. When bay 1 is full, monitor with a compost thermometer and turn into bay 2 when the temperature drops. Bay 1 is then free to receive a new batch.
What happens to the bales

After two to three years, the bales on the outer face will have begun to decompose. At this point, replace them with fresh bales and use the old bale material as a carbon addition to a new heap, or as mulch on paths. The partially composted bale straw is a valuable soil amendment in its own right.

attrs.fix: Cover the top of the structure with a tarpaulin after each loading to protect the bale tops from saturation. Barley straw lasts longer wet than wheat.
attrs.cause: Wet weather has soaked the bales from above; the strings holding the bales have degraded
attrs.symptom: Bales are collapsing after the first winter

attrs.fix: A hot heap needs at least 1 m³ of material. If only one bay is loaded with a small amount, consider adding more material before attempting to hot-compost.
attrs.cause: Volume is insufficient for thermophilic conditions; heap is too small for the bale structure
attrs.symptom: Heap is not heating despite good C:N ratio and moisture
